core_behavior.note: Models agree on secure upload and storage but diverge on additional behaviors: Model B adds file retrieval/download, Model C adds quota enforcement, opaque storage keys, audit logging, and fail-fast size enforcement not present in A or B.

inputs.note: Model C specifies precise input fields with types, HTTP transport requirements, and encoding constraints (multipart/form-data, TLS version), while Models A and B describe inputs at a higher level without transport or encoding specifics.

outputs.note: Model C specifies detailed HTTP status codes and structured JSON error bodies; Models A and B describe outputs as generic success/failure messages and file identifiers without HTTP semantics.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes 100 MB max file size; Model B assumes 10 MB max file size; Model C lists no assumptions. Additionally, Models A and B specify different allowed file type sets.

failure_modes.note: Model C specifies structured HTTP error codes with rate limiting (429) and service unavailability (503) responses not present in A or B; Models A and B describe failure modes in natural language without HTTP codes.

edge_cases.note: Model A handles duplicate filenames by appending identifiers and allows empty files with a warning; Model B prevents zero-byte uploads as a security measure; Model C handles duplicate content via SHA-256 hash deduplication and chunked transfer.

security_constraints.note: Model C adds opaque storage key generation, isolated storage not publicly accessible by default, TLS version requirements, and per-user/tenant quotas not present in A or B; Model B adds input validation against SQL injection and XSS not mentioned by others.

performance_constraints.note: Model A specifies 100 concurrent uploads and 5-second processing time; Model B specifies minimum upload/download speeds of 1 MB/s and 100 GB storage capacity; Model C frames performance constraints around fail-fast size enforcement and rate limiting.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 5 of 8 fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement caused models to independently introduce different design choices across inputs, outputs, core behavior, edge cases, and performance constraints.
